chunks.title: Catalog Description / Additional Course Details / Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
Operating Systems. (3-0). Credit 3. Review of computer architecture hardware/software evolution leading to contemporary operating systems; basic operating systems concepts; methods of operating systems design and construction; algorithms for CPU scheduling memory and general resource allocation; process coordination and management; case studies of several operating systems; quality-of-services of operating systems and their impact on applications. Only one of the following will satisfy the requirements for a degree: CSCE 410 or CSCE 611. Prerequisites: CSCE 313; graduate classification.

This course gives an overview of the general architecture and the most important components of modern operating systems. The student will leverage knowledge acquired in previous courses on Computer Architecture and Computer Systems to understand (and to some level implement) aspects of operating systems, such as memory management, persistent storage and file systems, threading, scheduling, and resource management in general. The student will understand fundamental approaches to virtualization and what it takes to build a distributed system. The course will cover general aspects, realization approaches, and case studies. In addition, the student will implement a simple operating system that will be able to boot and run on a bare PC.

None

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Describe and compare the role, the architecture, and the major components of an operating system.
• Describe the role and operation of exception and interrupt handling in modern operating systems.
• Implement components of a virtual memory manager for a paged system.
• Evaluate and compare various forms of concurrency mechanisms.
• Evaluate and compare and apply various synchronization mechanisms, both for single-processor and multiprocessor systems.
• Implement components of a threading system for a single-processor system.
• Implement components of a simple device driver.
• Implement components of a simple file system.
• Evaluate and compare the various virtualization approaches and their effect on system performance.

Notes:
This book is available for free in PDF form. It can also be purchased as hardcopy.

chunks.title: Grading Policy / Submission of Assignments
chunks.content:
The course will have a midterm and a final exam (totaling 400 points). In addition, there will be a series of design and programming assignments (machine problems) that will walk the students through the steps of designing and developing a simple operating system (400 points). Finally, there will be a set of daily quizzes (100 points) and a set of homework assignments (100 points).

The course will be using a standard letter grading scale.

Standard Letter Grading Scale:
A = 90% and above
B = 80% and above
C = 70% and above
D = 60% and above
F = less than 60%

Note that the total score for daily quizzes and for homework assignments may not sum up to exactly 100 points each by the end of the semester, but rather be off by a few points. If this happens, we will re-weigh the total score at the end of the semester to make it sum up to 100 points. As a result, the total score as indicated by Canvas may be slightly off.

Homework assignments and machine problems will be posted and submitted on Canvas. It is the student's responsibility to make sure that the correct assignment is submitted to the correct place. Also, Canvas submissions may look completed when they actually are not. It is the student's responsibility to make sure that the submission process is completed. It is best to download the submission and to confirm that the submission stored on Canvas is the intended one.

Under no circumstances will the instructor team consider material that has not been part of the original submission. If a student wants to add to or correct submitted files because they noticed that the original submission was wrong or incomplete, they can do this, but this will be considered as a new submission, and any late penalty will apply. The instructor and the TA's will not consider non-submitted material, and they will not consider the file timestamps (as opposed to submission timestamps) as indication of completion of the assignment. (Time stamps of files can be easily tampered with and will therefore not be considered.) In some special cases we will consider GitHub timestamps to reconsider machine problem submissions.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy / Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

Deadlines for on-line quizzes and homework assignments will be hard. (Quizzes will disappear after the due date.) Late submissions will not be accepted. Unless noted otherwise, lateness for machine problems is penalized with 1/5 of the earned points per calendar day (1/3 during 10-week summer courses). Lateness penalty starts at the deadline and is pro-rated. This means that the student will incur a penalty of approximately 0.0139 percent for each minute that their submission is late (0.0231 percent during 10-week summer courses). No make-up work submissions will be accepted.

Note: Machine Problems are posted on Monday morning of the week where they are listed. They are due on Sunday night of the week where they are listed. MP7 is due on May 3. In order to allow for a timely grading, the due date for MP7 is hard.

chunks.title: Academic Integrity
chunks.content:
"An Aggie does not lie, cheat or steal, or tolerate those who do.”

The most important value of this course is in its power of "demystification": You will experience a number of "aha, this is how it works!" moments, which are most valuable, and enjoyed best, when you experience that discovery through your own work, rather than copying somebody else's.

You are encouraged to discuss solution approaches to programming assignments. The solution that you hand in has to be your own, however. This means that you are not to use any part (code, text, figures, etc.) that has been developed by others, including Al. Spelled out, this means:

• For programming assignments: Do not show or make available in any form to other students either design documents or code. Do not look at or use other students' design documents or code. Do not make use of previous years' solutions. Do not look at or download solutions from the Internet. Do not submit or make use of Al generated solutions.
• For homework: Do not make your homework available to other students. Do not copy from other students' work. Do not look at or download solutions from the Internet. Do not submit or make use of Al generated solutions.
• For exams: Do not use any information from sources other than the exam form and allowed cheat sheets; disallowed sources of information include the Internet, books, notes, Al tools, or other students. Do not pass any information to other students. By information we mean any information, not even the time-of-day.

How do we determine that plagiarism has happened? We will delegate our decisions to code-similarity detection tools and automated plagiarism detection tools. Whenever a tool flags your submission, we will treat it as a potential violation, and we will issue a report to the Aggie Honor System Office. So, to be safe, stay away from other students' code and other solutions that are not your own!

Once an alleged violation has been reported, it is Aggie Honor Systems Office policy to not allow the student to Q-drop the course until the student is cleared of the violation.

chunks.content:
CSCE 611 | Operating Systems | Spring 2026
Instructor: Riccardo Bettati | bettati@tamu.edu

Topics: x86 interrupts and exceptions, TLB, RAID, Flash Memory, UNIX File System, UNIX Fast File System, Journaled and Log-Based File Systems, Recursive Page Table Lookup, Event-based Concurrency, Kernel vs. User level Threads, Thread Dispatching, Atomic Transactions, Critical Section Problem (SW/HW Solutions), Paged System, Virtual Memory Mechanisms, Memory Virtualization, Segmentation, Address Spaces, Paging, Memory Allocation, CPU scheduling, resource allocation, process coordination, operating systems concepts, operating systems design, operating systems construction, concurrency mechanisms, synchronization mechanisms, threading systems, device drivers, file systems, computer architecture, hardware/software evolution, modern operating systems components, persistent storage, scheduling, virtualization, distributed systems, simple operating system implementation, system calls, I/O management
Tools: piazza, Canvas, GitHub
Prerequisites: CSCE 313; graduate classification
